worth_it_explanation: Custom Audiences are the warmest audiences a Meta account can target, and how they are built decides whether retargeting reaches recent visitors, whether customer lists match enough people to be useful, and whether uploaded data carries the consent the terms require. The checks read directly from Business Settings and the Audiences view, so confirming what exists, how the retention windows are set, and whether the customer data terms were accepted takes a short pass. Each finding points to a configuration change rather than a rebuild, and tightening these audiences improves match rates and retargeting reach from the next refresh.
intro: Custom Audiences let a Meta Ads account retarget people it already has a relationship with, built from website activity captured by the dataset, from uploaded customer lists, or from engagement on Meta's own surfaces. These checks confirm that the account is actually using website Custom Audiences and customer lists where they fit, that retention windows match the buying cycle rather than a default, and that any uploaded customer data carries the consent the Customer List Custom Audiences Terms require.

audit_checks.title: No website Custom Audiences or customer lists are in use
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Without website Custom Audiences or customer lists, the account cannot retarget recent visitors or reach known customers, and it loses the seed sources that lookalike audiences are built from. Spend that could reach a warm audience goes entirely to colder prospecting, which usually carries a higher cost per acquisition.

educate.base:
A Custom Audience is a Meta targeting source built from people an advertiser already has a relationship with. Two sources do most of the work for a demand-side account. A website Custom Audience is built from activity the Meta dataset records on the site, so it can hold people who viewed a product, added to cart, or started checkout in a recent window. A customer list Custom Audience is built from contact data the advertiser uploads, such as a hashed email or phone number, and matched against Meta accounts.

When neither source exists, the account has no warm audience to retarget and no first-party seed for lookalikes. Every impression is served to a prospecting audience that has shown no prior interest, and the cost per acquisition usually sits higher there than against people who already visited or bought. The account is also more exposed to signal loss since a customer list built from data the advertiser collected directly does not depend on the dataset firing on every page.

The presence of these audiences is visible in the Audiences view inside Business Settings. A few signs suggest this check applies:
  • The Audiences list shows no website Custom Audience, or only audiences that have stopped refreshing
  • No customer list has been uploaded, or the only lists are old one-off uploads for a past campaign
  • Active campaigns target interests and broad audiences with no warm audience in the mix
You may want to confirm whether the account has a reason to skip these sources, such as a strict policy on uploading customer data, before treating their absence as a gap.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a store, website Custom Audiences map cleanly to the funnel since the dataset already records product views, add-to-cart, and purchase as standard events. You may want to confirm the account has audiences for recent viewers and cart abandoners at minimum, and that a customer list of past buyers exists as a seed for lookalikes and for suppression of recent purchasers.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: On a lead-generation account, the warm sources are visitors who reached a form and the contact records the business already holds. You may want to confirm a website Custom Audience captures people who viewed the form or a high-intent page, and that a customer list of existing leads or customers is uploaded both as a lookalike seed and as a suppression list so prospecting does not pay to reach people who already converted.

investigate.intro: Open the Audiences view in Business Settings and confirm whether the account has active website Custom Audiences and at least one current customer list.

meta_business_suite.label: Meta Business Suite
steps: Open Business Settings, then go to the Audiences view for the ad account.
Note whether any website Custom Audience exists and whether its size is still growing, which indicates the dataset is feeding it.
Note whether any customer list Custom Audience exists and when it was last updated. A list uploaded once for a past campaign and never refreshed counts as stale rather than active.
Open a few active campaigns and check the audience each ad set targets. Record whether any warm Custom Audience is in use or whether targeting is interest and broad only.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records whether the account has an active website Custom Audience, whether it has a current customer list Custom Audience, and whether active campaigns target any warm audience. An account with at least one refreshing website Custom Audience and a current customer list, used in live campaigns, passes. An account with neither source, or with only stale audiences that no active campaign targets, is a fail recorded with what is present and what is missing.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record whether a website Custom Audience and a customer list exist and whether active campaigns use them to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}}'s Meta account is running without warm Custom Audiences in place. There is no active website Custom Audience to retarget recent visitors and no current customer list to reach known customers or seed lookalike audiences, so spend reaches prospecting audiences that have shown no prior interest, where the cost per acquisition usually runs higher. {{voice_recommend}} building a website Custom Audience for recent high-intent visitors, uploading a current customer list of past buyers or leads, and adding these warm sources to active campaigns alongside the existing prospecting targeting.

audit_checks.title: Website Custom Audience retention windows are too narrow for the buying cycle
audit_checks.impact_rationale: A retention window shorter than the time it takes a buyer to decide drops people out of the audience before they return to convert, so retargeting reaches a smaller pool than the traffic supports. The audience can also fall below the size Meta needs to serve it, which stops delivery entirely.

educate.base:
A website Custom Audience holds a person for a set number of days after the activity that qualified them, called the retention window. Meta defaults a new website Custom Audience to a 30-day window and allows up to 180 days. When the window passes, the person drops out of the audience unless they trigger the qualifying activity again.

A window set shorter than the real buying cycle removes people while they are still deciding. A considered purchase that takes six to eight weeks to close will lose its early researchers from a 30-day audience well before they come back to buy, so the retargeting reaches only the most recent slice of visitors. The reverse problem also exists. A window stretched to 180 days on a fast-moving impulse purchase fills the audience with people whose intent has gone cold, which dilutes the audience and the bidding that learns from it.

Audience size compounds the effect. Meta needs an audience to clear a minimum size before it will serve, so a narrow window on a low-traffic site can leave the audience too small to deliver at all. A few signs suggest the window needs attention:
  • High-value or considered-purchase audiences sit at the 30-day default with no adjustment for the longer cycle
  • A retargeting ad set shows little or no delivery, which can mean the audience fell below the serving threshold
  • Every website Custom Audience uses the same window regardless of the activity or the value of the purchase
You may want to set the window from the time it actually takes that audience's buyers to convert rather than from the default, and to size it against the traffic the qualifying activity generates.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a store, the right window depends on the price point and the consideration it demands. You may want to confirm that low-cost impulse products use a short window that keeps intent fresh, while higher-priced items that buyers research over weeks use a longer window so early researchers stay in the audience until they return. A single window applied to the whole catalog rarely fits both ends.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: On a lead-generation account, the cycle from first visit to a submitted form can run long for considered services, so a 30-day window may drop prospects before they convert. You may want to set the window to match the typical time from first touch to lead, and to confirm the resulting audience clears Meta's minimum serving size given the site's traffic.

learn_more.source_title: Create a custom audience from website events

investigate.intro: Review each website Custom Audience and compare its retention window against the buying cycle for the activity that qualifies people into it.

meta_business_suite.label: Meta Business Suite
steps: Open Business Settings, then the Audiences view, and select each website Custom Audience in turn.
Note the retention window in days and the activity that qualifies people, for example a product view, an add-to-cart, or a visit to a high-value page.
Note the current audience size. An audience that reads as too small to deliver is a sign the window or the qualifying activity is too narrow for the traffic.
Compare the window against how long buyers for that activity typically take to convert, using the account's own time-to-conversion if available.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per website Custom Audience, the retention window, the qualifying activity, the audience size, and the typical time to conversion for that activity. An audience whose window covers the buying cycle and whose size clears the serving threshold passes. An audience whose window ends before buyers typically convert, or whose size is too small to deliver, is a fail recorded with the audience name, its window, and the cycle it should cover.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the retention window, qualifying activity, and audience size for each website Custom Audience above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}}'s website Custom Audiences use retention windows that do not match the buying cycle. Windows set shorter than the time buyers take to convert drop people out of the audience before they return, so retargeting reaches a smaller pool than the traffic supports, and narrow audiences can fall below the size Meta needs to serve them. {{voice_recommend}} setting each audience's window from the actual time its buyers take to convert, lengthening windows for considered purchases and keeping them short for impulse ones, and confirming each audience still clears the minimum serving size.

audit_checks.title: Customer lists are uploaded without documented consent or a lawful basis
audit_checks.impact_rationale: The Customer List Custom Audiences Terms require the advertiser to warrant a lawful basis and consent for the contact data uploaded, and to honor opt-outs. Uploading without that basis exposes the advertiser to regulatory action under privacy law and to suspension of the account's audience tools, which would remove its warmest targeting.

educate.base:
A customer list Custom Audience is built by uploading contact data, such as a hashed email or phone number, that Meta matches against accounts. Before that upload, the advertiser accepts the Customer List Custom Audiences Terms, which require a representation that the advertiser has the rights, permissions, and a lawful basis to use the data for this purpose. The terms also require the advertiser to honor any opt-out a person has exercised and to remove that person from the audience.

The risk is that the data gets uploaded without the consent or lawful basis the terms assume. A list scraped, purchased from a third party, or collected for a different stated purpose does not carry consent for ad targeting on Meta. Under the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) and the California Privacy Rights Act (CPRA), using contact data this way without a lawful basis is a violation that can draw regulatory action, and it breaches Meta's terms, which can lead to suspension of the account's audience tools.

The audit cannot read a person's consent record from inside Meta, so this check looks at the source and the process behind each list. A few signs suggest the basis is not documented:
  • The list came from a purchase, a scrape, or a third party rather than the advertiser's own collection
  • The data was collected for a purpose that did not mention advertising or sharing with Meta
  • There is no process to remove people who opt out from future uploads of the list
You may want to confirm with the data owner that each uploaded list has a documented lawful basis and a working suppression process before relying on it since the warranty in the terms sits with the advertiser.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a store, the customer list usually comes from order and account records, where the basis is stronger if the privacy notice at signup or checkout covered advertising use. You may want to confirm that notice exists and that customers who unsubscribe or opt out are excluded from the list before each upload, rather than uploaded and left in.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: On a lead-generation account, lists are often built from form fills, gated content, or a customer relationship management system, and the basis depends on what the form's notice said at capture. You may want to confirm the capture notice covered advertising use and that opt-outs recorded in the system are removed before the list is uploaded to Meta.

learn_more.source_title: Prepare your data for a customer list custom audience

investigate.intro: For each customer list Custom Audience, trace where the data came from and confirm a documented lawful basis and a suppression process for opt-outs.

meta_business_suite.label: Meta Business Suite
steps: Open Business Settings, then the Audiences view, and list every customer list Custom Audience in the account along with its size and last update.
For each list, identify the source with the data owner, for example the store's own order records, a customer relationship management system, or a third-party list.
Ask whether the notice at the point the data was collected covered advertising use, including sharing a hashed identifier with Meta.
Ask whether opt-outs and unsubscribes are removed from the list before each upload, and whether that step is documented rather than ad hoc.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per customer list Custom Audience, the data source, whether the collection notice covered advertising use, and whether a suppression process removes opt-outs before upload. A list from the advertiser's own collection with a notice covering advertising use and a working suppression step passes. A list from a purchase, a scrape, or a third party, a list with no notice covering this use, or a list with no opt-out suppression is a fail recorded with the list name and the gap.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the source, the collection notice, and the opt-out suppression process for each customer list above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}} uploads customer lists to Meta without a documented lawful basis for the contact data. Meta's Customer List Custom Audiences Terms require the advertiser to warrant a lawful basis and consent for the data and to honor opt-outs, and uploading lists that lack that basis exposes the account to regulatory action under privacy law and to suspension of its audience tools. {{voice_recommend}} confirming with the data owner that each list comes from a source whose collection notice covered advertising use, removing opt-outs before every upload, and retiring any list that cannot show a documented basis.
